The **2019 South Asian Games**, officially the **XIII [South Asian Games](/wiki/South_Asian_Games "South Asian Games")**, was a major [multi-sport](/wiki/Multi-sport "Multi-sport") event which was originally slated to be held from 9 to 18 March 2019 in [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu") and [Pokhara](/wiki/Pokhara "Pokhara"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[4]](#cite_note-venues-4) However, the dates were postponed and the event was held from 1–10 December 2019, coincidentally at the same time as the [2019 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2019_Southeast_Asian_Games "2019 Southeast Asian Games") in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The new dates were confirmed at the South Asian Olympic Council Executive Board meeting in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") on 1 March 2019. The [Dasarath Stadium](/wiki/Dasarath_Rangasala_Stadium "Dasarath Rangasala Stadium") hosted the opening ceremony along with the [men's football tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_2019_South_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2019 South Asian Games – Men's tournament"), with the stadium's renovation after the [2015 earthquake](/wiki/April_2015_Nepal_earthquake "April 2015 Nepal earthquake") completed in under 10 months with an increased capacity of 20,000,[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) along with the closing ceremony on 10 December.

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Venues")]

Three different cities in Nepal hosted the competition: [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu"), [Pokhara](/wiki/Pokhara "Pokhara") and [Janakpur](/wiki/Janakpur "Janakpur").[[4]](#cite_note-venues-4)

## Logo and mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Logo and mascot")]

On 13 May 2019, the [Ministry of Youth and Sports of Nepal](/wiki/Ministry_of_Youth_and_Sports_(Nepal) "Ministry of Youth and Sports (Nepal)") unveiled the logo and mascot of the 13th South Asian Games. The ministry along with the [Nepal Olympic Committee](/wiki/Nepal_Olympic_Committee "Nepal Olympic Committee") revealed a pair of [blackbucks](/wiki/Blackbuck "Blackbuck") (*Krishnashar*) as the official mascot for the 2019 South Asian Games. Blackbucks are the only extant member of the [genus Antelope](/wiki/Antelope "Antelope") and mostly found in the southern region of Nepal and is an endangered species with in Nepal.[[10]](#cite_note-sag_2019_logo-10)[[1]](#cite_note-opening-1)

The committee also revealed the official logo of the Games, a flying pigeon that embodies the vibrant colour of the logo of [South Asia Olympic Council](/wiki/South_Asia_Olympic_Council "South Asia Olympic Council"). It also includes world heritage sites and mountains of Nepal in the background.[[10]](#cite_note-sag_2019_logo-10)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Games")]

### Participating nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Participating nations")]

Seven countries competed. For the first time since [1999](/wiki/1999_South_Asian_Games "1999 South Asian Games"), Afghanistan did not compete at the South Asian Games, after leaving the [South Asia Olympic Council](/wiki/South_Asia_Olympic_Council "South Asia Olympic Council") in 2016. A total of 2,715 athletes competed.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sports")]

These Games featured 26 sports[[11]](#cite_note-11) with cricket returning after 8 years.[[12]](#cite_note-katpost-12) Golf and karate were the two sports added by the hosts as their choice.[[12]](#cite_note-katpost-12) All events must include a minimum of four participating teams otherwise it will not be held. ## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Records")]

1. On 2 December 2019, [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_women%27s_national_cricket_team "Nepal women's national cricket team") women cricketer [Anjali Chand](/wiki/Anjali_Chand "Anjali Chand") created history by registering best bowling figures in any international [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") match. She took 6 [wickets](/wiki/Wicket#Putting_down_a_wicket "Wicket") for 0 [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") while playing against [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_women%27s_national_cricket_team "Maldives women's national cricket team").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
2. On 3 December 2019, Indian shooter [Mehuli Ghosh](/wiki/Mehuli_Ghosh "Mehuli Ghosh") clinched the [10m air rifle](/w/index.php?title=Shooting_at_the_2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shooting at the 2019 South Asian Games (page does not exist)") gold with a score of 253.3 in the final. Though her score, which is 0.4 more than the current world record of 252.9, created by another Indian shooter [Apurvi Chandela](/wiki/Apurvi_Chandela "Apurvi Chandela") is not recognised by the international body [ISSF](/wiki/International_Shooting_Sport_Federation "International Shooting Sport Federation") but is the Games record.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
3. Indian high jumper Sarvesh Anil Kushare created Games record in the final of the [high jump](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2019_South_Asian_Games "Athletics at the 2019 South Asian Games") event. He jumped a height of 2.21 meter breaking 2.20 meters set by Sri Lankan athlete [Manjula Kumara](/wiki/Manjula_Kumara "Manjula Kumara") at the [2004](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2004_South_Asian_Games "Athletics at the 2004 South Asian Games") edition.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
4. On 5 December 2019, Karthik Unnikrishnan of India broke the previous Games' [Triple jump](/wiki/Triple_jump "Triple jump") record of 16.45 metres set by Indian [Renjith Maheshwary](/wiki/Renjith_Maheshwary "Renjith Maheshwary") in the previous edition. He jumped a distance of 16.47 metres to win the gold medal.
5. On 6 December 2019, [2018](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2018_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_shot_put "Athletics at the 2018 Asian Games – Men's shot put") Asian Games champion, [Tejinder Pal Singh](/wiki/Tejinder_Pal_Singh_Toor "Tejinder Pal Singh Toor") set a new Games record in [Shot put](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2019_South_Asian_Games "Athletics at the 2019 South Asian Games"). He threw the shot at a distance of 20.03 metres breaking previous record of 19.15 metres set by another Indian athlete [Bahadur Singh Sagoo](/wiki/Bahadur_Singh_Sagoo "Bahadur Singh Sagoo") in the 1999 edition.[[19]](#cite_note-19) On the same day, Sri Lankan men's [4x100 m relay](/wiki/4_%C3%97_100_metres_relay "4 × 100 metres relay") team consisting of [Himasha Eashan](/wiki/Himasha_Eashan "Himasha Eashan"), Chanuka Sandeepa, [Vinoj De Silva](/wiki/Vinoj_De_Silva "Vinoj De Silva"), [Yupun Priyadarshana](/wiki/Yupun_Priyadarshana "Yupun Priyadarshana"), set a new Games record, timing 39.14 secs bettering 39.91 secs previously set by Indian team in the [2004](/wiki/2004_South_Asian_Games "2004 South Asian Games") edition.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
6. On 7 December 2019, Pakistani athlete [Arshad Nadeem](/wiki/Arshad_Nadeem "Arshad Nadeem") created a new Games record in [Javelin throw](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw"). He threw a distance of 86.23 metres bettering previous distance of 83.23 metres by India.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Broadcasting rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Broadcasting rights")]

Organizer of 2019 South Asian Games, Nepal Olympic Committee (NOC), sold the broadcast rights to A company, NK Media Ventures Pvt Ltd who ensured that the event will be available live in more than 15 channels across South Asia. In Nepal, following channel purchased broadcasting rights from NK Media Ventures.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

* [Nepal Television](/wiki/Nepal_Television "Nepal Television")
* [AP1 TV](/wiki/AP1_TV "AP1 TV")
* Action Sports channel

## Doping at the 2019 South Asian Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2019_South_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Doping at the 2019 South Asian Games")]

150 random athletes participating in SAG 2019 were tested for Prohibited substances and the test samples were sent to [WADA (World Anti-Doping Agency)](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency")- accredited laboratory in Qatar by **SAG Anti Doping Organizing Committee**. 3 Athletes had failed the Doping Test. They appealed against the result of the test; they were given another chance to get a fair test and were called off with B-samples. The test held in the same laboratory and they had failed the test again. They were banned for four years until 2 December 2023.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

"As per the international norms of athletics, the trio will be stripped of their medals and the athletes finishing just behind them will be given their medals," said RK Bista, International Technical Officers of Athletes.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
